question: Your API stores each user's draft order in server memory keyed by a session cookie. Which REST constraint does this violate?
explanation: Statelessness. Requests only work on the server holding the session, forcing sticky sessions and breaking horizontal scaling and failover. Fix: make the draft a resource (GET/PUT /users/{id}/draft-order) so any server can handle any request.

question: An API has URIs like /orders/7 but only accepts POST with {"action": "delete"} bodies. What Richardson maturity level is it?
explanation: Level 1: it has resources (one URI per thing) but doesn't use HTTP verbs. Reaching level 2 means DELETE /orders/7 -> 204, GET /orders/7 -> 200/404, using methods and status codes properly.

question: Support staff need to query orders across all users. Which is the better canonical route?
explanation: When an entity has its own identity and is queried across parents, promote it to a top-level resource with a filter. Deep or redundant nesting (carrying a user_id that order IDs already make unique) is decoration, not information.

question: You need a 'cancel order' operation that isn't a clean field update. What's the preferred REST-friendly model?
explanation: Model imperative transitions as a controller sub-resource POST (POST /orders/7/cancel) with its own body, permissions, and audit trail — what Stripe and GitHub do. Never invent custom HTTP methods, and never use GET to mutate state.
